title: Prostate Cancer
oneLiner: Most common male cancer in UK; ranges from indolent (active surveillance) to aggressive (metastatic lethal); high prevalence & variable prognosis demands risk-adapted management.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Spinal cord compression** – *Acute back pain + bilateral leg weakness/sensory loss/bowel or bladder dysfunction.* Needs MRC grading, MRI spine within 4 hours, dexamethasone 16mg IV stat, urgent oncological spine team referral. Do NOT wait for imaging if cauda equina syndrome (saddle anaesthesia, painless retention).
- **Hypercalcaemia of malignancy** – *Severe bony metastases + dehydration + confusion/abdominal pain/polyuria.* Check corrected calcium urgently. Give IV fluids (0.9% saline 1–2L over 4h), IV bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid 4mg IV or pamidronate 90mg IV) after fluids, monitor K+, Mg²⁺. This is a medical emergency.
- **Bilateral ureteric obstruction** – *Oliguric acute kidney injury with flank pain or anuria, often painless.* Check U&Es, renal USS (hydronephrosis). Urgent urology +/- nephrostomy if creatinine >300 μmol/L and obstructed.
sections.Presentation:
- **Lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS):** frequency, urgency, nocturia, hesitancy, weak stream, incomplete emptying (non‑specific, may be BPH)
- **Haematuria** – macroscopic or microscopic
- **Bone pain** – lower back, pelvis, hips, ribs (metastatic disease)
- **Unexplained weight loss, fatigue, anaemia** – advanced or metastatic
- **Acute urinary retention** – painful or painless (elderly)
- **Erectile dysfunction / loss of libido** (rare as presenting symptom)

**Key exam findings:**
- **Digital rectal exam (DRE):** firm, irregular, asymmetrical prostate; loss of median sulcus; fixation to pelvic sidewall suggests T3/T4.
- **Spinal tenderness** – metastatic deposit
- **Lymphadenopathy** – supraclavicular (virchow’s node) or inguinal
- **Prostate size** – often not enlarged in early disease (BPH is larger, smooth)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH)** – >50% of men over 60; symmetrical, smooth, enlarged on DRE; PSA normal or mildly elevated (but can overlap)
- **Prostatitis** – acute: fever, perineal pain, tender prostate; chronic: recurrent UTIs, lower back/pelvic pain; PSA may be elevated (transiently)
- **Bladder cancer** – painless haematuria ± clumps; normal DRE; urine cytology + cystoscopy definitive
- **Metastatic disease of unknown primary** – bone pain, weight loss; PSA >100 ng/mL strongly suggests prostate primary; may need MRI prostate + biopsy
- **Urinary tract infection** – dysuria, frequency, urgency; nitrites/leucocytes on dipstick; low PSA unless chronic prostatitis

**Splitter:** *A man over 50 with LUTS + normal DRE + PSA 4–10 ng/mL → likely BPH; BUT same patient with firm/asymmetrical DRE + PSA >10 → prostate cancer until proven otherwise.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
    - DRE (document size, consistency, symmetry, sulcus)
    - Urine dipstick (exclude UTI, haematuria)
    - IPSS score for LUTS severity (optional)
- **Bloods:**
    - **PSA** – age‑adjusted: <50: ≤2.5; 50–59: ≤3.5; 60–69: ≤4.5; >70: ≤6.5 ng/mL. Any rise is abnormal; trend matters more than single value.
    - **FBC, U&Es, LFTs, bone profile (Ca²⁺, PO₄, ALP)** – check for metastatic disease
    - **Testosterone level** – baseline before androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)
- **Imaging:**
    - **First-line (if raised PSA +/or abnormal DRE):** multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) of prostate – PIRADS scoring (1–5); PIRADS ≥3 → target biopsy
    - **Second-line (if MRI contraindicated or negative but high suspicion):** transperineal/transrectal ultrasound‑guided biopsy (with antibiotic prophylaxis)
    - **Bone scan (Tc‑99m)** – if Gleason ≥7, PSA >20, or symptoms of bone mets
    - **CT chest/abdomen/pelvis** – if high‑risk or metastatic disease on bone scan
- **Special tests:**
    - **Prostate biopsy histology:** Gleason score (3+3=6, 3+4=7, 4+3=7, 4+4=8, etc.) – risk stratification
    - **PSMA-PET/CT** – if PSA progression after primary treatment (biochemical recurrence)
    - **Germline testing** – for BRCA1/2 if strong family history or metastatic disease
- **Diagnostic criteria/staging:**
    - **Risk groups (NICE CG175):** low (PSA <10, Gleason ≤6, T1–T2a); intermediate (PSA 10–20, Gleason 7, T2b); high (PSA >20, Gleason 8–10, T2c–T4). Also consider metastatic status (M0 vs M1).
- **Never‑miss test:** **Whole‑spine MRI** in any patient with new back pain + known prostate cancer (rule out cord compression).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Spinal cord compression – dexamethasone 16mg IV stat, MRI spine within 4h, urgent oncology/spine referral
    - Hypercalcaemia – IV fluids + IV bisphosphonate after fluids
    - Acute urinary retention – catheterise (Foley 14Fr), check U&Es, USS renal/bladder
    - If new diagnosis of metastatic disease – start ADT (LHRH agonist) or surgical castration after discussing multi‑disciplinary team (MDT)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Confirm staging (mpMRI, bone scan, CT) if not done
    - Start ADT for high‑risk locally advanced or metastatic (LHRH agonist like leuprorelin acetate [Prostap] 11.25mg SC every 12 weeks OR degarelix 240mg SC initially then 80mg monthly)
    - Consider combined ADT + bicalutamide 50mg PO OD to prevent flare (especially if symptomatic locally advanced)
    - Pain management – WHO ladder: paracetamol, NSAIDs (ensure renal function adequate), strong opioids (e.g. morphine immediate‑release 5–10mg PO 4‑hourly) titrate up
    - Start oral bisphosphonate (alendronic acid 70mg PO weekly) or IV zoledronic acid for bone mets to reduce skeletal‑related events
    - Monitor PSA, U&Es, Ca²⁺, ALP
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - **Active surveillance:** low‑risk (Gleason ≤6, PSA <10, T1–T2a) – repeat PSA 6‑monthly, mpMRI at 1 year, re‑biopsy at 3–5 years
    - **Radiotherapy (EBRT) + ADT:** intermediate/high‑risk localised
    - **Radical prostatectomy:** low‑intermediate risk, often robotic
    - **Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist)** – lifelong for M1 disease; monitor for osteoporosis, hot flushes, fatigue, loss of libido
    - **Chemotherapy (docetaxel)** – metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) or high‑volume metastatic hormone‑sensitive disease
    - **Abiraterone + prednisolone** or **enzalutamide** – after progression on ADT
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** *N/A for routine prostate cancer – unless acute complication (retention, cord compression, hypercalcaemia). Then:*
    - IV fluids: 0.9% saline 1L/8h if nil by mouth or hypercalcaemic
    - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if not bleeding)
    - Diet: NBM until voiding assessed post‑catheter; otherwise normal
    - Observations: 4‑hourly NEWS2 for acute metabolic/neurological instability, otherwise daily
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line ADT (LHRH agonist):**
    - Leuprorelin acetate (Prostap) – 11.25mg SC every 12 weeks (also 1‑monthly 3.75mg available)
    - Goserelin (Zoladex) – 3.6mg SC every 28 days OR 10.8mg every 12 weeks
    - Triptorelin – 11.25mg IM every 12 weeks
- **Alternative (LHRH antagonist):**
    - Degarelix (Firmagon) – 240mg SC (two 120mg injections) loading dose then 80mg SC monthly
    - Place: no flare reaction, useful in imminent cord compression or severe metastatic pain
- **Anti‑androgen (to block flare with LHRH agonist):**
    - Bicalutamide (Casodex) – 50mg PO OD; start 7 days before first LHRH dose
- **Bisphosphonates:**
    - Alendronic acid – 70mg PO once weekly; contraindicated if CrCl <35 mL/min or hypocalcaemia; give with full glass water, stay upright 30 min
    - Zoledronic acid – 4mg IV over 15 min every 3–4 weeks; monitor Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, PO₄, renal function
- **Denosumab (Prolia)** – 120mg SC every 4 weeks (not first‑line, for CRPC)
- **Key interactions:** ADT prolongs QT interval; avoid combination with drugs that also prolong QT (e.g. amiodarone, methadone, certain antipsychotics)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** bicalutamide – no major dose change in renal impairment; degarelix – no dose adjustment, but limited evidence in severe hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving a **LHRH agonist without anti‑androgen cover** in a patient with impending spinal cord compression or significant metastatic bone pain – **flare phenomenon** (testosterone surge in first 1–2 weeks) can acutely worsen pain, cause paralysis, or even tumour lysis. Always co‑prescribe bicalutamide for at least 7 days before/same day.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - Spinal cord compression (as above)
    - Hypercalcaemia of malignancy (as above)
    - Acute urinary retention
    - Flare reaction after LHRH agonist (worsening pain, urinary obstruction)
    - Tumour lysis syndrome (rare, primarily in bulky metastatic disease after ADT – monitor K⁺, PO₄, urate)
- **Chronic:**
    - Androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) effects: hot flushes, erectile dysfunction, loss of libido, fatigue, sarcopenia, osteoporosis, increased fracture risk, metabolic syndrome (weight gain, insulin resistance), cognitive changes
    - Anti‑androgens: gynaecomastia, breast pain (consider prophylactic breast radiotherapy)
    - Radiotherapy: radiation cystitis, proctitis, bowel urgency; later erectile dysfunction
    - Chemotherapy (docetaxel): neutropenia, peripheral neuropathy, fatigue, alopecia
sections.Prognosis:
- **Low‑risk (Gleason ≤6, PSA <10, T1‑T2a):** 95% 20‑year cancer‑specific survival with active surveillance. Most die with, not from, prostate cancer.
- **High‑risk localised (Gleason ≥8, PSA >20):** 50–70% progression‑free at 5 years with radical therapy.
- **Metastatic (M1) at diagnosis:** median survival 3–5 years with modern ADT + docs; 30–40% live >5 years. CRPC stage: median survival 1–2 years with newer agents (abiraterone, enzalutamide).
- **Biochemical recurrence only (rising PSA after primary treatment):** often years later; re‑staging (PSMA-PET) guides salvage therapy.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always calculate and document Gleason score + risk group** – this drives treatment decisions.
- **Start bone‑protective therapy (bisphosphonate or denosumab) in any patient starting long‑term ADT** – reduce fracture risk and skeletal events.
- **Check testosterone level before starting ADT** – if low, could be “castrate‑naïve” and you need baseline to monitor.
- **Use the National Prostate Cancer Audit (NPCA) guidelines** for treatment pathways.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not start ADT without checking PSA + staging (CT, bone scan, MRI)** – missing synchronous visceral mets would change management (e.g., androgen deprivation + docetaxel if high‑volume).
- **Do not treat an elevated PSA with oral antibiotics (e.g., ciprofloxacin) for “prostatitis”** – this is poor practice; only use antibiotics if there’s clinical evidence of infection (fever, tender prostate, positive urine culture). Delay in cancer diagnosis is harmful.
- **Do not prescribe ADT for very low‑risk disease (Gleason 6, PSA <10)** unless patient has extreme symptoms – active surveillance is superior quality‑of‑life strategy.
- **Do not forget anti‑androgen cover (bicalutamide) when initiating LHRH agonist** – flare can cause devastating acute pain or paralysis.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** new or worsening back pain with leg weakness, numbness, or bladder/bowel control loss (cord compression); inability to pass urine; severe bone pain not controlled by simple analgesia; fever or rigors (especially if on catheter or biopsy).
- **Follow‑up:** All new prostate cancer patients should be discussed at MDT. Outpatient urology clinic in 2–4 weeks after staging results. PSA monitoring planned by MDT (usually 3–6 months). If on ADT, nurse‑led monitoring for side effects.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥5 or any red‑flag symptom** (see Killers First) – call reg/consultant immediately.
- **Suspected spinal cord compression** – call oncology registrar, refer to acute oncology service, arrange urgent MRI spine (<4 hours). If cauda equina syndrome (saddle anaesthesia, painless retention, loss of anal tone) – emergency neurosurgery referral.
- **Hypercalcaemia (corrected calcium >3.0 mmol/L)** – call medical registrar, IV fluids + bisphosphonate, monitor ECG (short QT). If refractory, consider calcitonin or haemodialysis.
- **Acute kidney injury (creatinine >300 μmol/L or urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 hours)** – call renal/urology team; nephrostomy or JJ stent if obstruction.
- **Significant sepsis (temperature >38°C, NEWS ≥7) in context of recent biopsy or catheter** – treat as urosepsis – blood cultures, IV antibiotics (e.g. gentamicin + co‑amoxiclav), urgent urology referral.

rawMarkdown:
## Prostate Cancer

**One-liner:** Most common male cancer in UK; ranges from indolent (active surveillance) to aggressive (metastatic lethal); high prevalence & variable prognosis demands risk-adapted management.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Spinal cord compression** – *Acute back pain + bilateral leg weakness/sensory loss/bowel or bladder dysfunction.* Needs MRC grading, MRI spine within 4 hours, dexamethasone 16mg IV stat, urgent oncological spine team referral. Do NOT wait for imaging if cauda equina syndrome (saddle anaesthesia, painless retention).
- **Hypercalcaemia of malignancy** – *Severe bony metastases + dehydration + confusion/abdominal pain/polyuria.* Check corrected calcium urgently. Give IV fluids (0.9% saline 1–2L over 4h), IV bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid 4mg IV or pamidronate 90mg IV) after fluids, monitor K+, Mg²⁺. This is a medical emergency.
- **Bilateral ureteric obstruction** – *Oliguric acute kidney injury with flank pain or anuria, often painless.* Check U&Es, renal USS (hydronephrosis). Urgent urology +/- nephrostomy if creatinine >300 μmol/L and obstructed.

### Presentation
- **Lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS):** frequency, urgency, nocturia, hesitancy, weak stream, incomplete emptying (non‑specific, may be BPH)
- **Haematuria** – macroscopic or microscopic
- **Bone pain** – lower back, pelvis, hips, ribs (metastatic disease)
- **Unexplained weight loss, fatigue, anaemia** – advanced or metastatic
- **Acute urinary retention** – painful or painless (elderly)
- **Erectile dysfunction / loss of libido** (rare as presenting symptom)

**Key exam findings:**
- **Digital rectal exam (DRE):** firm, irregular, asymmetrical prostate; loss of median sulcus; fixation to pelvic sidewall suggests T3/T4.
- **Spinal tenderness** – metastatic deposit
- **Lymphadenopathy** – supraclavicular (virchow’s node) or inguinal
- **Prostate size** – often not enlarged in early disease (BPH is larger, smooth)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH)** – >50% of men over 60; symmetrical, smooth, enlarged on DRE; PSA normal or mildly elevated (but can overlap)
- **Prostatitis** – acute: fever, perineal pain, tender prostate; chronic: recurrent UTIs, lower back/pelvic pain; PSA may be elevated (transiently)
- **Bladder cancer** – painless haematuria ± clumps; normal DRE; urine cytology + cystoscopy definitive
- **Metastatic disease of unknown primary** – bone pain, weight loss; PSA >100 ng/mL strongly suggests prostate primary; may need MRI prostate + biopsy
- **Urinary tract infection** – dysuria, frequency, urgency; nitrites/leucocytes on dipstick; low PSA unless chronic prostatitis

**Splitter:** *A man over 50 with LUTS + normal DRE + PSA 4–10 ng/mL → likely BPH; BUT same patient with firm/asymmetrical DRE + PSA >10 → prostate cancer until proven otherwise.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
    - DRE (document size, consistency, symmetry, sulcus)
    - Urine dipstick (exclude UTI, haematuria)
    - IPSS score for LUTS severity (optional)
- **Bloods:**
    - **PSA** – age‑adjusted: <50: ≤2.5; 50–59: ≤3.5; 60–69: ≤4.5; >70: ≤6.5 ng/mL. Any rise is abnormal; trend matters more than single value.
    - **FBC, U&Es, LFTs, bone profile (Ca²⁺, PO₄, ALP)** – check for metastatic disease
    - **Testosterone level** – baseline before androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)
- **Imaging:**
    - **First-line (if raised PSA +/or abnormal DRE):** multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) of prostate – PIRADS scoring (1–5); PIRADS ≥3 → target biopsy
    - **Second-line (if MRI contraindicated or negative but high suspicion):** transperineal/transrectal ultrasound‑guided biopsy (with antibiotic prophylaxis)
    - **Bone scan (Tc‑99m)** – if Gleason ≥7, PSA >20, or symptoms of bone mets
    - **CT chest/abdomen/pelvis** – if high‑risk or metastatic disease on bone scan
- **Special tests:**
    - **Prostate biopsy histology:** Gleason score (3+3=6, 3+4=7, 4+3=7, 4+4=8, etc.) – risk stratification
    - **PSMA-PET/CT** – if PSA progression after primary treatment (biochemical recurrence)
    - **Germline testing** – for BRCA1/2 if strong family history or metastatic disease
- **Diagnostic criteria/staging:**
    - **Risk groups (NICE CG175):** low (PSA <10, Gleason ≤6, T1–T2a); intermediate (PSA 10–20, Gleason 7, T2b); high (PSA >20, Gleason 8–10, T2c–T4). Also consider metastatic status (M0 vs M1).
- **Never‑miss test:** **Whole‑spine MRI** in any patient with new back pain + known prostate cancer (rule out cord compression).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Spinal cord compression – dexamethasone 16mg IV stat, MRI spine within 4h, urgent oncology/spine referral
    - Hypercalcaemia – IV fluids + IV bisphosphonate after fluids
    - Acute urinary retention – catheterise (Foley 14Fr), check U&Es, USS renal/bladder
    - If new diagnosis of metastatic disease – start ADT (LHRH agonist) or surgical castration after discussing multi‑disciplinary team (MDT)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Confirm staging (mpMRI, bone scan, CT) if not done
    - Start ADT for high‑risk locally advanced or metastatic (LHRH agonist like leuprorelin acetate [Prostap] 11.25mg SC every 12 weeks OR degarelix 240mg SC initially then 80mg monthly)
    - Consider combined ADT + bicalutamide 50mg PO OD to prevent flare (especially if symptomatic locally advanced)
    - Pain management – WHO ladder: paracetamol, NSAIDs (ensure renal function adequate), strong opioids (e.g. morphine immediate‑release 5–10mg PO 4‑hourly) titrate up
    - Start oral bisphosphonate (alendronic acid 70mg PO weekly) or IV zoledronic acid for bone mets to reduce skeletal‑related events
    - Monitor PSA, U&Es, Ca²⁺, ALP
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - **Active surveillance:** low‑risk (Gleason ≤6, PSA <10, T1–T2a) – repeat PSA 6‑monthly, mpMRI at 1 year, re‑biopsy at 3–5 years
    - **Radiotherapy (EBRT) + ADT:** intermediate/high‑risk localised
    - **Radical prostatectomy:** low‑intermediate risk, often robotic
    - **Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist)** – lifelong for M1 disease; monitor for osteoporosis, hot flushes, fatigue, loss of libido
    - **Chemotherapy (docetaxel)** – metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) or high‑volume metastatic hormone‑sensitive disease
    - **Abiraterone + prednisolone** or **enzalutamide** – after progression on ADT
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** *N/A for routine prostate cancer – unless acute complication (retention, cord compression, hypercalcaemia). Then:*
    - IV fluids: 0.9% saline 1L/8h if nil by mouth or hypercalcaemic
    - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if not bleeding)
    - Diet: NBM until voiding assessed post‑catheter; otherwise normal
    - Observations: 4‑hourly NEWS2 for acute metabolic/neurological instability, otherwise daily

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line ADT (LHRH agonist):**
    - Leuprorelin acetate (Prostap) – 11.25mg SC every 12 weeks (also 1‑monthly 3.75mg available)
    - Goserelin (Zoladex) – 3.6mg SC every 28 days OR 10.8mg every 12 weeks
    - Triptorelin – 11.25mg IM every 12 weeks
- **Alternative (LHRH antagonist):**
    - Degarelix (Firmagon) – 240mg SC (two 120mg injections) loading dose then 80mg SC monthly
    - Place: no flare reaction, useful in imminent cord compression or severe metastatic pain
- **Anti‑androgen (to block flare with LHRH agonist):**
    - Bicalutamide (Casodex) – 50mg PO OD; start 7 days before first LHRH dose
- **Bisphosphonates:**
    - Alendronic acid – 70mg PO once weekly; contraindicated if CrCl <35 mL/min or hypocalcaemia; give with full glass water, stay upright 30 min
    - Zoledronic acid – 4mg IV over 15 min every 3–4 weeks; monitor Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, PO₄, renal function
- **Denosumab (Prolia)** – 120mg SC every 4 weeks (not first‑line, for CRPC)
- **Key interactions:** ADT prolongs QT interval; avoid combination with drugs that also prolong QT (e.g. amiodarone, methadone, certain antipsychotics)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** bicalutamide – no major dose change in renal impairment; degarelix – no dose adjustment, but limited evidence in severe hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving a **LHRH agonist without anti‑androgen cover** in a patient with impending spinal cord compression or significant metastatic bone pain – **flare phenomenon** (testosterone surge in first 1–2 weeks) can acutely worsen pain, cause paralysis, or even tumour lysis. Always co‑prescribe bicalutamide for at least 7 days before/same day.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - Spinal cord compression (as above)
    - Hypercalcaemia of malignancy (as above)
    - Acute urinary retention
    - Flare reaction after LHRH agonist (worsening pain, urinary obstruction)
    - Tumour lysis syndrome (rare, primarily in bulky metastatic disease after ADT – monitor K⁺, PO₄, urate)
- **Chronic:**
    - Androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) effects: hot flushes, erectile dysfunction, loss of libido, fatigue, sarcopenia, osteoporosis, increased fracture risk, metabolic syndrome (weight gain, insulin resistance), cognitive changes
    - Anti‑androgens: gynaecomastia, breast pain (consider prophylactic breast radiotherapy)
    - Radiotherapy: radiation cystitis, proctitis, bowel urgency; later erectile dysfunction
    - Chemotherapy (docetaxel): neutropenia, peripheral neuropathy, fatigue, alopecia

### Prognosis
- **Low‑risk (Gleason ≤6, PSA <10, T1‑T2a):** 95% 20‑year cancer‑specific survival with active surveillance. Most die with, not from, prostate cancer.
- **High‑risk localised (Gleason ≥8, PSA >20):** 50–70% progression‑free at 5 years with radical therapy.
- **Metastatic (M1) at diagnosis:** median survival 3–5 years with modern ADT + docs; 30–40% live >5 years. CRPC stage: median survival 1–2 years with newer agents (abiraterone, enzalutamide).
- **Biochemical recurrence only (rising PSA after primary treatment):** often years later; re‑staging (PSMA-PET) guides salvage therapy.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always calculate and document Gleason score + risk group** – this drives treatment decisions.
- **Start bone‑protective therapy (bisphosphonate or denosumab) in any patient starting long‑term ADT** – reduce fracture risk and skeletal events.
- **Check testosterone level before starting ADT** – if low, could be “castrate‑naïve” and you need baseline to monitor.
- **Use the National Prostate Cancer Audit (NPCA) guidelines** for treatment pathways.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not start ADT without checking PSA + staging (CT, bone scan, MRI)** – missing synchronous visceral mets would change management (e.g., androgen deprivation + docetaxel if high‑volume).
- **Do not treat an elevated PSA with oral antibiotics (e.g., ciprofloxacin) for “prostatitis”** – this is poor practice; only use antibiotics if there’s clinical evidence of infection (fever, tender prostate, positive urine culture). Delay in cancer diagnosis is harmful.
- **Do not prescribe ADT for very low‑risk disease (Gleason 6, PSA <10)** unless patient has extreme symptoms – active surveillance is superior quality‑of‑life strategy.
- **Do not forget anti‑androgen cover (bicalutamide) when initiating LHRH agonist** – flare can cause devastating acute pain or paralysis.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** new or worsening back pain with leg weakness, numbness, or bladder/bowel control loss (cord compression); inability to pass urine; severe bone pain not controlled by simple analgesia; fever or rigors (especially if on catheter or biopsy).
- **Follow‑up:** All new prostate cancer patients should be discussed at MDT. Outpatient urology clinic in 2–4 weeks after staging results. PSA monitoring planned by MDT (usually 3–6 months). If on ADT, nurse‑led monitoring for side effects.

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥5 or any red‑flag symptom** (see Killers First) – call reg/consultant immediately.
- **Suspected spinal cord compression** – call oncology registrar, refer to acute oncology service, arrange urgent MRI spine (<4 hours). If cauda equina syndrome (saddle anaesthesia, painless retention, loss of anal tone) – emergency neurosurgery referral.
- **Hypercalcaemia (corrected calcium >3.0 mmol/L)** – call medical registrar, IV fluids + bisphosphonate, monitor ECG (short QT). If refractory, consider calcitonin or haemodialysis.
- **Acute kidney injury (creatinine >300 μmol/L or urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 hours)** – call renal/urology team; nephrostomy or JJ stent if obstruction.
- **Significant sepsis (temperature >38°C, NEWS ≥7) in context of recent biopsy or catheter** – treat as urosepsis – blood cultures, IV antibiotics (e.g. gentamicin + co‑amoxiclav), urgent urology referral.
